Question: Explain what is wrong with the following statements. Use complete sentences. a) If there is a 60 percent chance of rain on Saturday and a 70 percent chance of rain on Sunday, then there is a 130 percent chance of rain over the weekend. b) The probability that a baseball player hits a home run is greater than the probability that he gets a successful hit.
Answer: a) You can't calculate the joint probability knowing the probability of both events occurring, which is not in the information given; the probabilities should be multiplied, not added; and probability is never greater than 100 percent b) A home run by definition is a successful hit, so he has to have at least as many successful hits as home runs.
o3 Answer:
a) The 60 % and 70 % figures refer to two different events, “it rains on Saturday” and “it rains on Sunday.”  
These events are not mutually exclusive—rain could occur on both days—so you cannot obtain the probability of rain on at least one day by simply adding the two numbers.  The correct relationship is  

 P(rain on the weekend) = P(rain Sat ∪ rain Sun)  
          = P(rain Sat) + P(rain Sun) − P(rain Sat ∩ rain Sun),  

and this result can never exceed 100 %.  Saying there is a 130 % chance of rain therefore violates the basic rule that probabilities are numbers between 0 and 1 (0 % and 100 %).

b) Every home run counts as a hit, so the event “the player hits a home run” is a subset of the event “the player gets a hit.”  
Because the probability of a subset cannot exceed the probability of the set that contains it, the probability of a home run must be less than or equal to the probability of any hit.  Hence it is impossible for the probability of a home run to be greater than the probability of getting a hit.
o4-mini Answer:
a) This statement treats the chances of rain on Saturday and Sunday as if they were counts that can simply be added, but probabilities are not counts and the events “rain on Saturday” and “rain on Sunday” are not mutually exclusive.  In fact, the probability of rain at least one day is  
P(rain Saturday or Sunday) = P(rain Sat) + P(rain Sun) – P(rain both days),  
so it cannot exceed 100 percent.  Saying there is a “130 percent chance” is therefore meaningless.  

b) A home run is one specific type of successful hit, so the event “hits a home run” is a subset of the event “gets a successful hit.”  Hence the probability of a home run cannot exceed the probability of a successful hit.

Question: After a mayor of a major Canadian city announced his plans to cut budget costs in late 2011, researchers polled 1,046 people to measure the mayor’s popularity. Everyone polled expressed either approval or disapproval. These are the results their poll produced: In early 2011, 60 percent of the population approved of the mayor's actions in office. In mid-2011, 57 percent of the population approved of his actions. Answer: a) The Forum Research surveyed 1,046 Torontonians. b) 58 percent c) 42 percent of 1,046 = 439 (rounding to the nearest integer) d) .57 e) .60.

   (We round to the nearest whole person.)

   Mid-2011 approval = 57% → probability = 0.57.

e)  Probability a randomly chosen person supported the mayor in early-2011  
   Early-2011 approval = 60% → probability = 0.60.
